blog_posts.title: the web
blog_posts.description: research, reactions, and thinking, all connected through a blog on mikeveson.com.
blog_posts.context: A living collection of Mike's research, philosophical analysis, and thinking, all connected like a web through a blog on mikeveson.com. Covers topics from the Tree of Human Flourishing to computer ethics to relationship philosophy. Established in 2026 as a public window into how Mike thinks.
blog_posts.summary: the web is research, reactions, and thinking, all connected through a blog on mikeveson.com. Posts cover ethics and technology, the Tree of Human Flourishing, relationship philosophy, and whatever Mike is currently processing. Each post can have its own visual identity through custom theming. It's a raw, informal window into how Mike thinks: sophisticated in substance, casual in presentation. Not polished for the sake of it. Everything is connected because that's how his brain works.

blog_posts.title: Human or Dancer
blog_posts.description: A dancer executes; a human expresses. The barrier AI is really lowering was never the barrier to making things — it's the barrier to knowing yourself. Now that execution is effortless, do we still bother to be human in what we make?
blog_posts.context: A reaction piece written after reading Luay Nakhleh's (Rice Dean of Engineering and Computing) article 'Is AI Ruining Our Ability to Think? The Wrong Question to Ask,' which came out of a Rice gathering, 'Human Flourishing in the Age of AI,' co-organized by Rodrigo Ferreira, who taught Mike's computer ethics class. The title riffs on The Killers' 'Human' ('Are we human, or are we dancer?') and Hunter S. Thompson's line about raising 'a generation of dancers': human = self-knowing, self-directed, expressing; dancer = moving to a rhythm someone else set, the passive consumer. The emotional core is Knight Life, the scheduling app Mike built in high school for himself to make the world in his head real — not as an entrepreneur — which only spread because his sister Natalie told her friends.
blog_posts.summary: Mike's central claim: there are three distinct struggles, and AI affects them differently. (1) The struggle to START — learning syntax, getting over the wall of not knowing how — is worth removing, and these tools remove it. (2) The struggle to CARVE — taking a rough vision and perfecting it until it's exactly what you saw — is the entire point and must never be skipped (the Kevin Parker reference: he made 'The Less I Know the Better' fast, then carved it for months). (3) The struggle to KNOW YOURSELF is the deepest and is Nakhleh's real answer: the struggle worth protecting was never learning Java loops, it was self-examination. So Mike is neither pro- nor anti-AI: he's for removing the start-struggle and keeping the carve and self-knowledge struggles. His film fear (he's seen Across the Spider-Verse five times) is not 'AI makes bad art' but that when the barrier to making drops to zero, people stop sitting with a vision long enough to perfect it. The conditional thesis: 'AI can be the most important piece in our journey toward flourishing' — only if it's pointed at self-knowledge, not consumption. The deferred, darker societal fear (who decides what gets built; consumption over self-knowledge) is named but intentionally left for a heavier future piece.

blog_posts.title: How Do I Not Feel This?
blog_posts.description: On the question almost everyone asks but very few sit with. Not 'how do I not feel this?' but 'why am I feeling this?' On silencers, catharsis, and the difference between reckoning and rebranding.
blog_posts.context: Personal companion to Mike's PHIL 150 philosophy paper of the same title. Traces his own 2025 collapse: multiple structures of identity falling apart at once, the forced reflection after an ankle sprain made every avoidance strategy physically unavailable, and the realization that self-knowledge is harder than it seems because most people don't want it. The post introduces a three-phase framework (pre-catharsis, at-catharsis, post-catharsis) and identifies the design gap in self-knowledge tools, including Apollo: they assume the user has already crossed the threshold of wanting to examine themselves. This is why Mike thinks Apollo works for people who've already done the will-to-look work, but doesn't reach the people who most need self-knowledge.
blog_posts.summary: Mike's most personal piece, written from one year out of a 2025 collapse he didn't initially want to look at. He argues the default human question is 'how do I not feel this?' (embodied, relief-seeking) and the question that actually changes you is 'why am I feeling this?' (slower, reflective, understanding-seeking). The post traces why almost everyone lives inside the first question and what it costs: silencers (achievement, busyness, social activity that prevent you from noticing examination is missing), the horrible-person fear (avoidance is protective, not indifferent; the sick feeling is evidence against being horrible), and the rebranding trap (catharsis without genuine recognition produces the most comfortable available story, which looks like recovery). Connects directly to the Olympus/Apollo design problem: every self-knowledge tool assumes the user wants to examine themselves, but most people who need self-knowledge most haven't reached that point. The closing line: 'the pursuit itself is the answer to the question.'

blog_posts.title: Our Unwavering Loyalty to the Wrong Thing
blog_posts.description: On confusing the container with what's inside it. Why we keep doing things long after the joy they were supposed to produce is gone.
blog_posts.context: Uses Mike's own evolution with soccer as the through-line. He loved playing soccer for the joy it produced (freedom, presence, running without thinking, connection). Over years of competitive play, the practice survived but the joy disappeared: he was playing for recognition, for outcomes, for the version of himself other people validated. The post generalizes the pattern: when we lose track of the ends that originally drew us to something, we end up unwaveringly loyal to the means. This is how lives get spent on activities that no longer serve their original purpose.
blog_posts.summary: A philosophical examination of means-end confusion using soccer as the central example. Mike loved soccer because of what it produced: joy, freedom, presence, connection, health. Not for the sport itself. But as he got better and more competitive, the activity outlived the joy: he was playing for outcomes, recognition, validation. The post argues this pattern is everywhere. When the joy or original purpose disappears from an activity, we don't usually notice. We just keep doing the activity. The container (the practice) gets confused with what was inside it (the actual flourishing it produced). The corrective is intuition and self-examination: noticing when the thing you do is no longer giving you what made it worth doing.

blog_posts.title: AI Companions and Self-Deception
blog_posts.description: The wrong question about AI companions is whether they can replace real relationships. The right one is whether you are honest with yourself about what you are actually getting from them.
blog_posts.context: Mike's first major essay on The Web. Introduces the 'silencers' concept that recurs throughout his later writing. Argues that the standard debate (are AI companions good or bad?) is the wrong frame. The actual issue: AI removes the friction that makes hard conversations hard, which is sometimes the point (catharsis benefits) and sometimes the problem (you bypass the work of being seen by another person). The piece also introduces Mike's view that we exist as slightly different characters within each relationship, built over hundreds of interactions, and AI is the first thing that lets you talk outside of all of them at once. That can be freeing or it can be self-deception. The difference is honesty about which one is happening.
blog_posts.summary: Mike argues the popular debate about AI companions (good or bad? can they replace real relationships?) misses the point. The real question is: what are you actually getting from your AI usage, and are you honest about it? He notes that humans exist as slightly different characters in each relationship, built over hundreds of interactions, and that breaking out of those characters with friends is socially risky. AI removes that friction entirely: no judgment, no social consequences. That can be valuable (a space to articulate what you can't say elsewhere) or it can be a silencer (a way to avoid the work of being known by another person). The piece introduces the 'silencers' concept that recurs through Mike's later writing: anything that prevents you from noticing that self-examination is missing.

blog_posts.title: What Are You Going to Do With Your Life?
blog_posts.description: Written for my freshman-year entrepreneurship class. I reflect on the spiral in my life caused by the intersection of my family's legacy and my own personal drive for innovation and technology.
blog_posts.context: Written for Mike's freshman-year entrepreneurship class at Rice. Reflects the intersection of his family's legacy in shipping software and his own personal drive for innovation and technology, and how that's influenced him.
blog_posts.summary: This post explores Mike's entrepreneurial journey, deeply influenced by a family history in shipping software. It covers how observing his parents build Veson Nautical from the ground up demystified the process of starting a company. The narrative connects his early exposure to tech to his hands-on experience developing the first Veson Nautical Mobile App, illustrating a full-circle moment that solidified his passion for building impactful software.
